rationale: This reaction shows the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile) to form an aryl ketone. This is the classic transformation for the Houben-Hoesch reaction, using a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a protic acid (HCl) as catalysts. The substrates are simple and acyclic where possible, clearly illustrating the core C-C bond formation and electrophilic aromatic substitution mechanism.
collision_avoidance_note: This example avoids using phenol (which could trigger Fries or Reimer-Tiemann queries) and uses a simple nitrile instead of more complex acylating agents. The use of a highly activated methoxy-substituted benzene ring is characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, distinguishing it from the more general Friedel-Crafts acylation which typically uses less activated arenes and acyl halides/anhydrides.
